speaker: interviewer
text: After you got the birth certificate with Sophia's name on it, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Was Sophia is in English. Zosha is in Polish. I went to Warsaw, which was just one station. And I bought a paper, newspaper. And I opened the section of Wanted, Help Wanted. And I saw a family with two children need a mother's helper. They didn't say a year. They didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked up-- after asking where it is, I walked up to this family. It was already towards evening. And I said, I read your ad in the paper, and here I am to be your mother's helper. And she said, but you're just a kid. I said, well, I can do the work. So but where are your parents? So I told them my parents are-- that was my luck, that this I thought of myself-- that my parents are in the forest fighting the Germans. It's Armia Krajowa. It's the AK, which means 'The Army of the Country.' And they couldn't take me with. So don't you have any grandparents? Any family? I said, nobody really could take me. So now, I am coming here to do the work. And she was desperate, because she needed-- they had a store, and she needed help, and the two kids, to cook, and clean, and wash. The kids were big enough to go to school themselves. It wasn't too far. So I-- I was there a short time. I don't know if I was there two weeks. And she came from the store one day. She says, you know, Zosha, there was a Polish family in this building-- I'm talking about the big block, the one and a yard, then another one and a yard, the three blocks-- there was a Polish family that was hiding Jews. The Germans came, and they killed the Polish family in their apartment along with the Jews. So if you are Jewish, don't stay here. Don't endanger us, and don't endanger yourself. So I said, I'm not Jewish. Here is my birth certificate. And it was lucky that I knew the catechism better than a Polish child. Because again, of my father's wisdom, when I was going to school back before the war, we used to have one hour a week for Jewish religion and one hour a week for the Polish religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a very inquisitive kid. I wanted to know what they are talking about. So one day, I came home-- because when they had religion, the Jewish kids played outside for an hour. And when we had, the Catholic kids played for an hour. But I didn't like to play an hour. So I asked my father. I said, you know, Daddy, I would love to sit in and hear what they are talking about. I know, even now, rabbis would never allow it to children to do that, because I worked in a Jewish school, so I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said, you really want to know? I said, yes, I do. Well, it's OK. Because he was sure, whatever I learned at home, that I-- I won't get converted. So I sat in on their hour too, and I learned the catechism. Now, I don't remember a thing. But at that time, when it came a test, they all gathered around me. Tell me. Tell me, Dorca. What's this mean? What does that mean? And I would tell them, but I didn't write the test. It's just that I was sitting in listening. So actually, I said, I'm not the-- Jewish, because that's the first thing they would ask a child when they caught them, to tell the catechism. Lots of Jewish people learned it. Like my sisters had to learn it later. But I was the only one that my father allowed, because the others didn't ask. I did. [SIGHS] But the next morning, when they went to work and the kids went to school, I didn't have even what to pack up. [LAUGHS] You left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just left. And I said, oh, my god. I can't go back to my sister. So I took another newspaper, and I looked for another mother's helper. And here, I come up-- I remember-- [NON-ENGLISH] What number? I don't know. It was on the seventh floor walk-up. 32, I think. And a lady opens the door. And I tell her that I came. I had the paper in my hand. But you're just a kid. The same story as she gave me before. And I tell the same story, that my parents couldn't take me. And she felt sorry for me. But I think she felt sorry more for herself, because she didn't have time to do the housework.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her, I know, but you'll see. I'll do all the work. Just try me out. I won't take money for a month. And she was very-- she needed me badly, you see? So she said, OK, I'll try you out for a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget. She says, first thing, do the dining room. And the dining room, she had French Provincial furniture that stay on legs, you know, that come out. I must have seen my sisters do it, or the helpers do it at home, that you move away-- it wasn't a china cabinet, which is the lower bottom. So I moved it away. And the son that was 14 years old that was helping her-- they had no carpets, just hardwood floors-- he moved all the dirt under. He didn't gather it. So when I moved away that commode, whatever you call it, there was heaps of dust there. And it looks like she looked what I'm doing. And she came to me. She said, Zosha, if you could do that, I know I can keep you. Oh, you can imagine how I felt. Finally, I got a place. She had also an old father-in-law. Her husband was a journalist. And they had a six-year-old girl and a 14-year-old boy, so-- with a dog, on the seventh floor walk-up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when was it that you went to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went soon after the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about what time was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just two weeks after, or something like that. I'm telling you, every day was like a year. So it was a short time, I know, that I came to this second one. And I was very anxious for her to keep me, because, my god, I can't go on looking for places. And I worked very hard, because it was big, big, a huge apartment. And I could never have done all the work. So I didn't sleep at nights. And I would do things that don't make noise, like ironing. You know, those days, they ironed pillowcases, sheets. You name it, and they ironed it. So I did that, the ironing, at night, the darning at night, some cooking at night. And so I slept very little, but I became very famous, again, in those big blocks, because of-- you know how women talk among themselves, that it's so much work, and day-to-day is too short. Everybody said, you go to Zo-- Zosha. She'll tell you how to manage. So when they came to me to ask me how I manage, I'm a kid, I said, you don't sleep at night. That's how you manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was frightened because-- it's funny that you asked that-- because about two weeks later, I was still frightened and working very hard. And I had a dream that, really, it-- [SIGHS] [SOB] I need a few minutes. [SOB] I saw my father-- I had a dream at night. And I didn't see his face, because we knew at the time that-- that they were burned. Treblinka. So I saw he had a gorgeous praying shawl with a silver, big tiara. And sometimes, the Jews, when they pray, really, and they don't want to see anything around them, they would cover their eyes. And he pulled over this shawl. And I recognized him. So I started crying, Daddy. I spoke to him in Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want to be here. Please take me. [SOBBING] And he says, my child, I cannot take you. It's not-- [SOB] it's not time for you to go. And he started backing up. I said, please take me. I don't want to be here. I'm scared. And he said, don't worry. I'll protect you. Whenever you be in danger, I'll save you. And I screamed. I said, no. And I spoke to him in Yiddish. I don't want to be here. And as I-- in the dream, I ran towards him-- he moved away. I could never touch him. And I screamed very hard. And the people came in. And I want you to know that Warsaw was built very well. It's not thin walls, like here. There were double doors, one close to the kitchen-- and there was a little hall-- and another one close to the dining room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came in, and they shook me a long time, she said, as I was screaming. But the first thing when I woke, I said, did you understand what I said? Because I was afraid that they would-- since I didn't speak Polish, I spoke Yiddish. They said, no, we didn't. So I was relieved, of course. And they said, what did you dream that was so terrible? So I told them, but not the whole truth, you see? I said, I dreamt about my father. And I wanted to go with him. And he wouldn't take me. And I screamed, please take me. [SOBBING] So that was it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after this, I was a little bit more reassured that-- as if he's watching over me. And I continued on working hard. I had to take the girl to the library and read to her, help her with the homework, and go to play in the park. Was very popular those days, cricket, you know, the English game. So I had to carry all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day-- the Polish people used to buy chicken alive, and they killed it themselves. So one day, I had to go to the market buy a chicken. It's alive. And who has to kill it? I have to. So there was a block in the-- in the second, like, courtyard. And there were a few Polish girls. So what did I do? I did like a [NON-ENGLISH] does. Takes it by the wings and put the head back. And they started yelling, Jews kill chicken like this. Oh, my god. Can you imagine? So I-- the chicken, of course, was tied. I dropped the chicken. I said, I can't do it. You do it for me. And she did. And then I said to this woman, this doctor I worked for, I can't do it. I can't kill chicken. Please do me a favor. You kill it, or ask somebody to kill it, or buy a dead one, or whatever. So that was it. But this old guy helped me very much, the father-in-law, because-- can you imagine seven floors, if I forgot something, to run down and up? So he devised a very nice idea. He made a basket with a string. [LAUGHS] And I would shout up what I forgot. And he would lower it to me, so I wouldn't have to run up seven flights and run down seven flights. But somehow, this didn't bother me, really, that much, because if you're young, you don't mind that. Now, the woman you worked for was a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was able to continue to work throughout the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she worked-- she was a gynecologist. She worked in the hospital through the wars. Nothing happened. And then a day before the uprising, the Polish uprising, they came and arrested her husband. And we never saw him since. Probably shot him, because he was the one that wrote in the newspaper in such a way it took a few days to decipher it, what the Germans, that they should start the Polish uprising. That's why they arrested. And they must have killed him, because we didn't hear from him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't that funny? I don't. I don't remember. I know the little girl was Eva. And the boy was Charlie. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Polish uprising was really very, very hard. I had to go and get water. There was a barricade. And you went with the pail down. They were shooting from the highest building in the area. [? Poznanzka. ?] It was a post office that overlooked the whole area.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many people got hurt and killed. And for some reason, I was lucky that I wasn't hurt at all. [BEEPING] Just pick it up, and put it down. Just pick it up. And so when they were loading-- the Germans were loading the cannons, we could hear it, because it was making woo, woo. We named it cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we heard this loading, everybody ran down to the basement. But guess what? I had so much work to do that I couldn't run down. So I went under the bed on the seventh floor. Can you imagine? The top floor of the building yet. And thank god that all the buildings were destroyed around, and ours wasn't. Well, you tell me. It's just a miracle. So when the uprising was won over by the Germans, they evacuated the whole city, Warsaw. They let us out just carrying small things by hand, towards evening yet, in the winter. Can you imagine? And this was the winter of '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. And we just went on the highway. The snow was up high on both sides. There were thousands and thousands of people going, just going. Nowhere to go. [SIGHS] But at night, when the little girl starts crying, she can't walk anymore, we carried her. We couldn't-- the old man was saying he can't walk anymore. So she noticed hospital on the other side. And she says, let's go there. Since I'm a doctor, maybe they'll help me. And sure enough, they did. We crossed over in the deep snow. And she said, these are my children. She didn't say I'm a helper anymore. I became her child. And this is my old father. She didn't even say, he's my father-in-law. And they took us in. They put us on the floor in the hallway. They gave us blankets, and pillows, and hot soup. And we slept there at night. That's where I am so cold. That's why I'm cold. That's why we moved from Canada, because it wasn't warm enough for me. That's why I don't like air conditioning either, because even when I go shopping near the frozen goods, right away, my legs hurt. And here, my-- my sinuses hurt too. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, they woke us up. And they gave the doctor money. And they say, you go to Krakow. And in Krakow, they'll help you, because they set up all kinds of helping care places for those peoples that were thrown out of Warsaw. And since she was a doctor, they would help even more, first. So we went to Krakow on the train. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they assigned her to a village. And I remember, my sister, Rachel, always said to me, she says, never go to a village, to a small place, because the people have lots of time. And they look at you in the eyes, and they look what you do and how you do. They see a holiday comes, if you get any Christmas cards, or any holiday greeting cards. She says, you have to always be in the big city. That's why my other sisters went to Warsaw, you see? But they got sick. They had typhus. So I had typhus too, but I was with a-- a family. Her kids were sick. And I was sick. So I was a kid, so I was only lucky that I didn't have my hair shaved off, because most of the people did. And I still retained my braids. Her kids' were shaved off, but I didn't. I don't remember if I didn't let her, or what.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she-- she was sent to go to a village. And I was sick that time. I was coughing badly. And they used to give me injections, gamma globulin, I think it was. So I had a very good excuse. I said, look, I can't go with you, because I'm sick. And I need these injections. So you will have to go alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do I doing now? I again take a newspaper. But I want you to know that she did not pay me money, this doctor. Never. And I was afraid to ask, because maybe she won't keep me if I ask. She'll have so much money to give me back pay. So I didn't ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the few-- the few cents I had to buy a newspaper. And I looked. And I saw that a professor and his wife need a mother's helper. Oh boy. I figured, now is my chance to get educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go to this place. I don't remember, for the life of me, what his name was. And I said, you know what? Let's make a deal. You won't pay me, but you will help me get my education. I finished five grades of high-- of public school, and you teach me the rest.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK. OK. That was fine. Was a very rundown house. Old. In Warsaw, it was a new complex. We didn't have any bugs. For the first time in my life, I was exposed to those big cockroaches. I-- I was afraid to put my foot in my shoe. I was afraid to sleep. There, I didn't have to work so hard, but I was afraid to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what what I doing? I need to choose, because I outgrew the shoes I had. So I was knitting for money. Whoever wanted something, I would knit, gloves, scarves, kerchiefs. Don't ask me how I knew what to do, how many stitches, and when to let down. It just was all in my head. [DRINKS] Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was saving money to buy shoes. And then, in '45, in the winter, when I already had money, oh, boy, I'm going to buy myself a new pair of shoes, the war broke out. The Russians came. You see, nobody expected that the war would start anywhere in the winter, for some reason. In Russia, we knew they started in the winter, the Russians, because they are used to it. And the German soldiers aren't. So here, the war started in the winter. And when the Russians came in, the money got devaluated. So I threw out the money. It was no good anymore. And I wanted to go home, but there were no trains running. Just for the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were essentially freed by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was free, and yet I wasn't, because where would I go? I stayed with the same family that he didn't teach me, he didn't pay me. I needed shoes. I wanted to go home, and I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had an idea one day. I'll go to the Russian command. Everybody knew where it is. And I would say that I am a Jew. But these people didn't know that I'm Jewish that I worked for. I said, I'm Jewish, and I am alone. I want to go and see if anybody survived. Could you please take me with you? So they said, sure. Come tomorrow in the morning. So I told the people, I'm going home. Again, I didn't have much to take with me. And I came to that station in the morning. I see a truck full-- by the time I was 14-- I see a truck full of soldiers. Men. I looked for a woman. And I asked the driver, is there any women on the truck? He said, no. They're only men. I said, sorry. I'm not going. I had enough sense not to go. So I don't want to go back to that professor. He didn't teach me. The cockroaches are just horrible. I couldn't sleep. So these people from Warsaw had a cousin in Krakow. So I went to them, and I told her that I don't have where to be, if I could work for her. And she took me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a gorgeous house that the Germans made headquarters in her house. It was a beautiful villa. Big. And the lady wasn't too young. She had two grown sons. They had a pharmacy. And I still don't know their name. Can't think of it, even. So I worked for her, very, very hard, because it was a huge house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did not know I'm Jewish. And to this day, I don't like French, because the doctor and her husband, all intelligentsia in Poland, spoke French. So it's not polite to talk at the table a language that you don't understand. But when they didn't want the children should understand, they would speak French. But I felt they are speaking about me, maybe, so I felt very uncomfortable in Warsaw and in Krakow, because they spoke French. And then I couldn't go home. There were no trains. So I had an idea. I remembered I had a sister in Israel. I knew her married name. But I knew she lives in Tel Aviv, but I didn't know the address. Didn't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka. So I wrote with a picture a-- a letter to the police station. And I told them I'm a survivor. And here is my picture to show. She'll recognize me. Would you please find my sister, Malka Koztrinski. Didn't remember her husband's name, even-- and tell her that I'm alive and where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- at the same time, I sent a letter to the house that I knew the Polish people, that didn't want to keep my other two sisters. And I-- for her, I didn't send a picture. And I said, please, Mrs. [? Koboz-- ?] I remembered her name too-- if anybody is alive from my family, would you please tell them, here is my address, that I am here? So my brother happened to pass by. And you know, in Europe, people-- women, mostly-- didn't work that time. So they sit by the window when they finish the work. She sits by the window on our street, next to our house-- that's my cousin's house from Australia, because we built the house with the semi-detached, that should cost cheaper. The uncle had half, and we had half. [NON-ENGLISH] she yelled out to him. I have a letter from your sister, Dorca.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he took the letter. But he couldn't go and get me, because he had to report to the [? Briha. ?] That was they were going [NON-ENGLISH] back to Israel. And my sister, Tofka, she had a baby while she was hidden. And-- but she didn't have it right in the house. She went to some Polish woman to deliver it. And another Polish woman that we knew picked it up. And she took it to Warsaw to the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the war, of course, my sister, Tofka, went with this [NON-ENGLISH] to the orphanage to find her son. And they said that after the Polish uprising, all the orphanage was shipped to Krakow. Near Krakow, there was a small place. And here I am, near Krakow. So she took that Polish woman with her, and she went first to find her baby. He would have been by then already, what? Three years? Two years old to two and 1/2? And then, she came to me. I didn't know she's coming, you see? All of the sudden, I see my sister. I recognized her. And I see this Polish woman. I could recognize her easily. She had a big red mark on her face like this, from fire, like-- looks like when her mother was pregnant, she must have done this. Oh, I'm glad to see you. And she said, pack your things. We're going. So I went to this woman. And I told her, this is my sister and our friend. And I'm going home. And of course, I didn't give her any notice, you see? I was working. So she said in Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH]. Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, like, this can only do the people from the East. She didn't want to say Jewish. See? She was a refined lady, a pharmacist's wife speaking French. So I-- we didn't pay any attention. And I said, I'm going home. And we left. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes now.
